name: Mobile Blood Collection – Within 2 miles
blurb: Our DBS-checked healthcare professional visits your home within 2 miles of Worthing. Includes all sterile equipment, centrifuge, and secure lab despatch.

name: Mobile Blood Collection – Within 5 miles
blurb: Same professional home-visit collection service covering up to 5 miles. Ideal for clients just outside the local zone.
components: As above – travel up to 5 miles from Worthing

name: Mobile Blood Collection – Within 10 miles / Anywhere UK
blurb: Widest standard mobile zone. UK-wide visits can be arranged. Remote areas may attract a small surcharge. NHS patients receive a £5 discount.
components: Up to 10 miles or anywhere UK. Remote/specialist areas: +£15–£40 surcharge. Under-13: ad-hoc pricing. NHS: £5 discount.

name: In-Clinic Blood Collection (Worthing)
blurb: Visit our Worthing clinic on the first Wednesday of each month. A qualified phlebotomist takes your sample in a calm clinical setting. £5 discount for NHS referrals.
components: Worthing BN14 7NB – one Wednesday per month. £5 NHS discount.

name: Basic Health Check Add-on
blurb: A simple blood pressure reading taken at your collection appointment, forwarded securely to your GP.
components: Blood pressure measurement sent directly to your GP

name: Enhanced Health Check Add-on
blurb: Blood pressure, height, and weight taken during your appointment and forwarded to your GP, providing a fuller baseline health picture.
components: height and weight – all sent directly to your GP

name: Legal Paternity Test – Standard Trio (Mother, Child & Father)
blurb: Court-admissible paternity test following strict chain-of-custody. DNA collected by a qualified HCP, sealed in tamper-proof packaging and sent to a Ministry of Justice accredited laboratory. Results are PhD-reviewed and legally valid for family court in England and Wales.
components: Child and Alleged Father. Chain-of-custody. MOJ-accredited lab (ISO/IEC 17025

name: Legal Paternity Test – Motherless (Child & Father only)
blurb: Same court-admissible legal paternity test without the mother's sample. Extra DNA markers compensate, maintaining the same high accuracy and legal validity.
components: Child and Alleged Father only. Chain-of-custody. HCP collection required. PhD-reviewed.

name: Peace-of-Mind Paternity Test – Standard (Mother, Child & Father)
blurb: At-home paternity test for personal reassurance. Not court-admissible. Kit posted to you; return swabs by post. Results reviewed by a PhD scientist. 99.99%+ probability if paternity confirmed.
components: Child and Alleged Father. Self-test kit. PhD-reviewed. Not for legal use.

name: Peace-of-Mind Paternity Test – Motherless (Child & Father only)
blurb: Private home paternity test without the mother. Extra markers tested to maintain accuracy. Not admissible in court.
components: Child and Alleged Father only. Self-test kit. PhD-reviewed.

name: DNA Identity Profile – Personal Record (Non-Legal)
blurb: Creates a permanent genetic identity record – a unique DNA fingerprint. Useful for genealogy, reconnecting with biological relatives, or personal identification records.
components: DNA Profiling YSTR or STR. No chain of custody. Self-test kit.

name: DNA Identity Profile – Legal Record (With Chain of Custody)
blurb: Legal DNA identity profiling with full chain-of-custody. Can be used for immigration applications, inheritance disputes, or official identity verification.
components: DNA Profiling YSTR or STR. Chain-of-custody collection. HCP collection required.

name: Baby Gender Reveal (Non-Invasive, from 8 weeks)
blurb: Find out your baby's sex as early as 8 weeks using a simple blood test analysing fetal DNA in maternal blood. No risk to mother or baby.
components: Non-invasive gender reveal from maternal blood. Self-test kit. Collection booked separately.

name: Heart Health Screen
blurb: Snapshot of cardiovascular health: checks cholesterol levels (LDL, HDL, triglycerides), long-term blood sugar (HbA1c), and high-sensitivity CRP (an inflammation marker linked to heart disease risk). In line with NICE guidance, early identification allows lifestyle changes or medication to cut heart attack and stroke risk.

name: Kidney Health Check
blurb: Measures waste products filtered by the kidneys (creatinine and urea) and calculates eGFR – the best measure of kidney filtration. NICE recommends monitoring in diabetes, hypertension, and family history of kidney disease.

name: Diabetes Risk Screen
blurb: HbA1c reflects average blood sugar over 2–3 months; more reliable than a single glucose. A random glucose is also measured. NICE: HbA1c ≥48 mmol/mol indicates diabetes. Early detection prevents complications including blindness, kidney damage, and nerve damage.

name: Full Blood Count (Complete Blood Picture)
blurb: One of the most common blood tests: examines red cells (oxygen carriers), white cells (infection fighters), and platelets (clotting). Can detect anaemia, infection, inflammation, bleeding disorders. Standard first-line NHS test for tiredness, breathlessness, and recurrent infections.

name: Cholesterol & Lipids Screen
blurb: Measures blood fats. LDL (bad cholesterol) builds up in arteries; HDL (good cholesterol) removes it; triglycerides are linked to heart and metabolic disease. NICE recommends lipid testing for cardiovascular risk factors or family history of early heart disease.

name: Salt & Kidney Balance (Urea & Electrolytes)
blurb: Checks key minerals and kidney waste products. Sodium/potassium balance is critical for heart rhythm and nerve function. Imbalances indicate dehydration, kidney disease, or medication effects (e.g. diuretics). One of the most-ordered NHS tests.

name: Urine Health Check (Microscopy – UMIC)
blurb: Detailed urine examination beyond a dipstick: looks for blood cells, casts (kidney damage), bacteria, glucose, and protein. Detects UTIs, kidney disease, diabetes, and liver problems. Standard NHS investigation for urinary symptoms.

name: Liver Function Test (LFT)
blurb: Checks liver health via enzymes and proteins. Raised ALT/AST/Gamma GT indicates cell damage from alcohol, fatty liver, hepatitis, or medication. Bilirubin measures jaundice. NICE recommends for anyone with liver symptoms or risk factors (obesity, alcohol use).

name: Thyroid Function Test (TFT)
blurb: Measures TSH (brain's signal to thyroid) and FT4 (main thyroid hormone). Raised TSH + low FT4 = underactive thyroid (hypothyroidism, 1 in 20 UK adults). Low TSH + high FT4 = overactive thyroid. Both easily treated. NICE: screen anyone with unexplained fatigue, weight changes, or mood disturbance.

name: Core Health Profile 1 (Vitamins, Blood, Liver, Kidneys)
blurb: Broad health screen: blood count, liver function, kidney function + electrolyte balance, vitamin B12, folate, and vitamin D. Suited to a general health MOT or investigating fatigue, brain fog, or recurrent infections.

name: Core Health Profile 2 (Blood, Sugar, Kidneys, Cholesterol, Liver)
blurb: Five-system screen: blood count, long-term blood sugar (HbA1c), kidney health, cholesterol, and liver enzymes. Efficient annual check or multi-risk-factor monitoring (diabetes, hypertension, high cholesterol).

name: Core Health Profile 3 (Kidneys, Cholesterol, Liver)
blurb: Focused three-system screen. Cost-effective for monitoring known conditions, routine medication reviews (statins require regular lipid and liver checks), or as a simple health baseline.

name: Well Man Check (Men's Comprehensive Health Screen)
blurb: Comprehensive annual check for men. Covers cardiovascular risk, diabetes, liver/kidney health, thyroid, testosterone, iron, key vitamins (B12, folate, vitamin D), and muscle health. Identifies early signs of the conditions most affecting men's long-term health.

name: Well Woman Check (Women's Comprehensive Health Screen)
blurb: Annual screen tailored to women. Adds key female hormones (FSH, LH, oestradiol) to assess hormonal balance and early menopause signs, plus iron (iron deficiency anaemia is the most common nutritional deficiency in women of reproductive age).

name: Fertility Hormone Profile (Women)
blurb: Assesses key hormones affecting a woman's ability to conceive. AMH measures ovarian reserve. Thyroid disorders are a common, treatable cause of fertility problems. Raised prolactin can suppress ovulation. Vitamin D deficiency is linked to reduced fertility.

name: Acute Medicine Profile 1
blurb: Broad acute-care panel: blood count, liver function, cholesterol, blood sugar, kidney/electrolyte balance, and bone minerals. Used in urgent or hospital-adjacent settings to rapidly assess multiple body systems.

name: Acute Medicine Profile 2 (Extended)
blurb: Extended acute panel adding iron stores, uric acid, CRP, muscle enzyme (CK), and full urinalysis. Provides comprehensive multi-organ assessment for acute presentations.

name: Lifestyle Health Screen
blurb: Lifestyle-focused panel combining standard organ function, cholesterol, blood sugar, and nutritional markers (vitamin D, B12, folate, iron). Ideal for anyone wanting to understand how their diet and lifestyle affect their health.

name: Basic Health Screen
blurb: Solid general health baseline: blood count, kidney function, liver enzymes, protein levels, cholesterol, and hs-CRP inflammation marker. Good starting point for a general health review.

name: Standard Health Screen
blurb: Comprehensive screen combining blood and urine analysis: metabolic health, organ function, inflammation, iron stores, vitamin D. Covers most areas of NHS health checks. Appropriate for an annual health MOT.

name: Standard Screen Plus – Women
blurb: Standard screen enhanced for women with full thyroid panel (including antibodies for autoimmune thyroid disease) and CA125 (ovarian cancer marker recommended by NICE when ovarian cancer suspected).

name: Standard Screen Plus – Men
blurb: Standard screen enhanced for men with full thyroid antibody panel and PSA (prostate health marker). PSA is the primary screening marker for prostate disease – the most common cancer in UK men.

name: Fatigue & Tiredness Investigation
blurb: Investigates persistent tiredness. Covers all major blood-test causes of fatigue: anaemia, thyroid disease (including autoimmune), diabetes and insulin resistance, kidney function, vitamin D, B12, folate, and magnesium deficiency. Mirrors NICE/NHS guidance for unexplained fatigue investigation.

name: Long Covid Recovery Panel
blurb: Specialist Long Covid panel investigating cardiac damage (Troponin I, CKMB, myoglobin), clotting abnormalities (D-dimers), inflammation, liver/kidney function, thyroid, and nutritional deficiencies. Supports GP discussions about ongoing management.

name: Muscle & Joint Disease Screen (Rheumatology)
blurb: Focused rheumatology and muscle screen. Rheumatoid factor for RA, uric acid for gout, immunoglobulins for inflammatory/autoimmune conditions. CRP for active inflammation. Vitamin D and magnesium deficiency are common contributors to muscle pain.

name: Gut & Nutritional Health Screen
blurb: Comprehensive gut and nutritional screen. tTG-IgA is the primary NICE-recommended test for coeliac disease. H. pylori antibodies screen for the bacterium causing peptic ulcers. Full nutritional status assessment alongside metabolic health.

name: Advanced Thyroid Panel (with Antibodies)
blurb: More detailed than a standard TFT. Adds Free T3 and two antibody tests (TPO, Thyroglobulin Abs), identifying autoimmune thyroid conditions (Hashimoto's, Graves') that a basic TSH/FT4 may miss. NICE recommends antibody testing when autoimmune thyroid disease is suspected.

name: Urine Culture & Sensitivity (Infection Test)
blurb: Full urine analysis including culture to identify bacteria and antibiotic sensitivity. Gold-standard NHS test for UTIs. Dipstick alone is insufficient to guide antibiotic prescribing. Also identifies non-infectious causes of urinary symptoms.

name: Bone Health Assessment
blurb: Assesses key markers of bone health. Option A: standard calcium and vitamin D screen. Option B: adds PTH and phosphate for investigating osteoporosis, hyperparathyroidism, or bone pain. NICE recommends vitamin D and calcium checks in osteoporosis risk groups.

name: Anaemia Investigation Panel
blurb: Targeted panel for anaemia investigation. FBC characterises the anaemia type; iron, B12, and folate address the most common causes; CRP distinguishes iron deficiency from anaemia of chronic disease.

name: Diabetes Status & Insulin Resistance Check
blurb: Goes beyond a simple diabetes screen to assess insulin resistance (HOMA-IR). C-peptide distinguishes type 1 from type 2 diabetes. Valuable for those with PCOS, obesity, or family history of diabetes, in line with NICE metabolic health guidance.

name: Coeliac Disease & Gut Screen
blurb: Primary blood test for coeliac disease (NICE recommended). tTG-IgA is most sensitive and specific. Total IgA included as IgA deficiency causes false-negatives. Test before starting a gluten-free diet.

name: Iron Level & Storage Test
blurb: Detailed iron panel: ferritin (best measure of stores), serum iron, transferrin (iron carrier protein), TIBC, and transferrin saturation. Distinguishes iron deficiency, iron overload (haemochromatosis), and anaemia of chronic disease. NICE: check before starting iron supplements.

name: Full Hormone & Endocrine Profile
blurb: Comprehensive endocrine panel: thyroid, pituitary, and reproductive hormones in one test. For hormonal symptoms including irregular periods, infertility, low sex drive, weight changes, fatigue, and mood changes.

name: Hormonal Health Panel (Female Hormones)
blurb: Assesses key female reproductive hormones. Option A: concise hormonal picture for cycle monitoring or initial investigation of irregular periods. Option B: fuller picture for PCOS, fertility concerns, low libido, or hormonal imbalance.

name: Stress Hormones (Cortisol & DHEA)
blurb: Measures the two main adrenal stress hormones. Chronically high cortisol links to weight gain, poor sleep, anxiety, and immune suppression. Low DHEA is associated with ageing and chronic stress. Provides insight into adrenal function and physiological stress load.

name: Complete Fertility Panel
blurb: One of our most comprehensive panels for fertility investigations. Covers every major hormonal and nutritional factor known to influence conception: reproductive hormones, thyroid, insulin resistance, stress hormones, vitamin D, B12, folate, and iron. Aligns with NICE fertility guidance.

name: Testosterone Level Check
blurb: Measures testosterone plus the proteins that carry it (SHBG, albumin), allowing calculation of biologically active free testosterone. Appropriate for investigating low sex drive, erectile dysfunction, fatigue, and muscle loss. Aligns with NICE guidance on testosterone measurement.

name: Pancreas Health Check
blurb: Checks pancreatic enzymes: raised amylase/lipase indicates pancreatitis or pancreatic damage. Relevant for upper abdominal pain, nausea, gallstones, or heavy alcohol use. NICE/hospital guidelines: primary blood test for suspected pancreatitis.

name: Muscle, Joint & Gout Screen
blurb: Focused panel for joint and muscle symptoms: uric acid for gout; rheumatoid factor as primary screening test for RA; CK elevated when muscle tissue is damaged or inflamed.

name: Erectile Dysfunction Blood Profile
blurb: Investigates the most common blood-test causes of erectile dysfunction per NICE guidance: testosterone, thyroid, HbA1c (diabetes), cholesterol (cardiovascular risk), and prolactin. PSA included if prostate disease is relevant.

name: Well Man Check 40+ (with PSA)
blurb: Annual health screen for men aged 40+, adding PSA to the standard Well Man panel. After 40, risks of prostate disease, cardiovascular conditions, and hormone changes increase. PSA testing from age 50 (or 45 with risk factors) is supported by NICE.

name: Menopause Blood Test
blurb: Measures hormones that change during menopause. NICE: in women over 45 with symptoms, diagnosis can often be made clinically; blood tests are useful under 45 or for atypical presentations. Thyroid function is included as thyroid disorders mimic many menopausal symptoms.

name: Ovarian Reserve Test (Egg Count Indicator)
blurb: Measures ovarian reserve. AMH is the most reliable marker of remaining egg supply. Used routinely before IVF treatment to guide drug protocols. Does not predict chance of natural conception.

name: MMR Immunity Check (Measles, Mumps, Rubella)
blurb: Checks protective antibodies against measles, mumps, and rubella. Important for healthcare workers, women planning pregnancy (rubella in early pregnancy risks fetal harm), and those unsure of vaccination history. NHS recommends rubella immunity screening in early pregnancy.

name: Connective Tissue Disease Screen
blurb: Investigates connective tissue diseases (lupus, Sjögren's, polymyositis, systemic sclerosis, MCTD). Recommended by NICE/British Society for Rheumatology when CTD suspected.

name: ENA Antibody Screen (Connective Tissue Antibodies)
blurb: Extractable nuclear antigen antibodies for specific CTD subtyping. Used when ANA is positive and a more specific diagnosis is needed.

name: Auto-Antibody Screen (Organ-Specific Autoimmune)
blurb: Screens for organ-specific autoimmune conditions: AMA (primary biliary cholangitis), smooth muscle and LKM Abs (autoimmune hepatitis), gastric parietal cell Abs (pernicious anaemia), TPO Abs (autoimmune thyroid disease).

name: Auto-Antibody Panel 2 (Endocrine & Organ Autoimmunity)
blurb: Specialist endocrine autoimmune panel. Islet cell Abs for type 1 diabetes; adrenal Abs for Addison's disease; ovarian/testicular Abs for autoimmune fertility conditions.

name: Basic Vitamins & Minerals Panel
blurb: Practical nutritional baseline: iron (most common nutritional deficiency worldwide), vitamin D (deficiency affects ~1 in 5 in UK), vitamin B12, folate, and magnesium/zinc. Good starting point for anyone concerned about nutritional status.

name: Full Vitamin B Complex Panel
blurb: Comprehensive B vitamin assessment. Relevant for vegans/vegetarians, digestive conditions, alcohol dependency, or medications interfering with B vitamin metabolism (e.g. metformin). Specialist laboratory processing accounts for the extended turnaround.

name: Detailed Vitamin D Status (D2 & D3 Breakdown)
blurb: Distinguishes D3 (from sunlight/animal foods) from D2 (from plants/some supplements). Useful for monitoring response to D2 vs D3 supplementation. Essential for bones, muscles, immune function, and mood.

name: Fat-Soluble & Key Vitamin Profile (A, B, C, E)
blurb: Extensive micronutrient panel covering vitamins A, B, C, and E. These act as antioxidants, support immune function, and are vital for skin and eye health. Relevant for malabsorption conditions, eating disorders, or very restrictive diets.

name: Heavy Metal Toxicity Screen
blurb: Tests for heavy metal accumulation and trace element status within red blood cells (reflecting long-term cellular levels). Relevant for occupational exposures, contaminated water, certain fish consumption (mercury), and industrial environments.

name: Core Vitamins Check (B12, Folate, Vitamin D)
blurb: Checks the three vitamins most commonly deficient in the UK: vitamin D (1 in 5 adults), vitamin B12 (especially vegans, older adults, people on metformin), and folate. Active B12 is more sensitive than total B12, detecting deficiency earlier.

name: Vitamins & Iron Check (B12, Folate, Iron, Vitamin D)
blurb: Combines key vitamin deficiency tests with a full iron profile. Particularly useful for women, vegans/vegetarians, athletes, and anyone with persistent fatigue. Iron deficiency and vitamin D deficiency are the two most common nutritional deficiencies worldwide.

name: Vitamins, Inflammation & Heart Risk Screen
blurb: Combines nutritional markers with cardiovascular and inflammation risk assessment. hs-CRP is an early indicator of arterial inflammation. Cholesterol profiles cardiovascular risk directly. Suits people interested in heart health prevention alongside nutritional optimisation.

name: Elite Sports Performance Panel
blurb: Comprehensive performance and wellness panel for athletes: oxygen-carrying capacity, muscle health/damage, hormonal optimisation, cardiovascular health, antioxidant capacity, and nutritional completeness. Results inform training load management and recovery strategies.

name: Food & Pet Sensitivity Genetic Test
blurb: Analyses DNA to identify genetic predispositions for common food intolerances and sensitivities. Note: a positive genetic predisposition does not always mean you will experience symptoms. Useful to guide elimination diets and nutritional planning.

name: Omega Fatty Acid Profile (Cell Health & Inflammation)
blurb: Measures the balance of omega-3 and omega-6 fatty acids within red blood cells. A healthy omega-3:6 ratio is anti-inflammatory; modern Western diets are typically low in omega-3. Low omega-3 linked to cardiovascular disease, depression, and cognitive decline.

name: Wellbeing Profile 1 (Energy, Heart & Hormones)
blurb: Wellbeing panel combining cardiovascular markers, metabolic health, stress hormones, and nutritional deficiencies. Cortisol and DHEA provide insight into adrenal function. Nutritional markers address the most common correctable causes of low energy.

name: Wellbeing Profile 2 (Core Health Markers)
blurb: Streamlined wellbeing panel covering inflammation, iron, organ function, cholesterol, and key vitamins. Good for regular monitoring or annual reviews.

name: Wellbeing Profile 3 (Comprehensive with Hormones)
blurb: Comprehensive health and hormone panel including Lipoprotein A (emerging cardiovascular risk marker). Advanced thyroid with antibodies adds depth. Suitable for thorough annual review or hormonal investigation.

name: Wellbeing Profile 4 (Hormone & Metabolic Focus)
blurb: Shares the comprehensive marker set as Wellbeing Profile 3. Full-spectrum metabolic, cardiovascular, hormonal, and nutritional health markers with advanced lipid risk stratification.

name: Wellbeing Profile 5 (Women's Advanced Hormone & Health)
blurb: Women-oriented wellbeing panel adding stress hormones (cortisol, DHEA), prolactin (suppresses ovulation when raised), and magnesium (linked to PMS, migraines, and sleep quality) to the core wellness markers.

name: Food & Inhalant Allergy Panel – 60+ Allergens (IgE)
blurb: Comprehensive IgE allergy panel testing 59+ food and environmental allergens. Covers all 14 major EU allergens plus common inhalant triggers. Results must be interpreted alongside clinical history. NICE: allergy testing should accompany a clear history of allergic symptoms.
components: Foods (30+ including all 14 EU major allergens)

name: Extended Allergy Panel – 80+ Allergens (IgE + Total IgE)
blurb: Widest allergy panel testing 80+ individual allergens plus Total IgE. Extended allergen list adds Candida, further tree pollens, additional grasses, and more foods. For people with multiple or complex allergy symptoms.

name: Extended Food Intolerance Panel – 90+ Foods (IgG)
blurb: Large food intolerance panel measuring IgG antibody reactions to 90+ foods. Associated with delayed food intolerance symptoms (hours to days after eating). Guides elimination diets. Note: IgG testing identifies potential sensitivities, not true IgE allergies.

name: Food Allergy Panel 34 – IgE (34 Foods)
blurb: IgE allergy blood test measuring immune reactions to 34 specific foods. Covers all 14 major EU food allergens. A positive result indicates sensitisation; must be interpreted with symptoms. Can identify causes of reactions from mild (itching, hives) to severe (anaphylaxis).

name: Food Allergy Panel 20 – IgE (20 Common Foods)
blurb: IgE reactions to 20 of the most commonly allergenic foods, including all major EU-regulated allergens. Practical first-line investigation for suspected food allergies. Results must be interpreted alongside clinical history (NICE guidance).

name: Dairy & Milk Allergy Panel (IgE)
blurb: Detailed panel for cow's milk and dairy allergies, including reactions to different dairy products and proteins (casein, whey). CMA is one of the most common food allergies in children. Results identify exactly which dairy components trigger immune reactions.

name: Inhalant Allergy Panel – Environmental Allergens (IgE)
blurb: Tests for IgE reactions to common environmental allergens you breathe in. Includes tree pollens, moulds, house dust mites, and pet danders. These cause hay fever, asthma, and perennial allergic rhinitis. Identifies specific triggers to guide avoidance and allergen immunotherapy.

name: Nut Allergy Profile – 13 Nuts (IgE)
blurb: Tests IgE responses to 13 nuts and seeds. Nut allergies are among the most severe food allergies (responsible for most food anaphylaxis deaths). Identifies specific nut triggers for safe avoidance and meal planning. NICE recommends specialist allergy assessment for suspected nut allergy.

name: Shellfish Allergy Panel – 8 Shellfish (IgE)
blurb: Tests IgE reactions to 8 common shellfish. Shellfish allergy often persists lifelong in adults. Reactions range from oral allergy syndrome to severe anaphylaxis. Distinguishes reactions to crustaceans (shrimp, crab, lobster) vs molluscs (mussel, oyster, clam, scallop).

name: Complete STI Screen (14 pathogens)
blurb: Most comprehensive STI screen: 14 pathogens by PCR and antibody tests. Covers HIV, hepatitis B and C, syphilis, herpes, gonorrhoea, chlamydia, and rarer infections. NICE/BASHH recommend regular STI screening for sexually active people. Many STIs are curable if treated early.
components: HIV I & II Ab/p24 Ag screen

name: STI Profile 1 – Blood-Borne Viruses (HIV & Hepatitis)
blurb: Checks for the three most important blood-borne viruses: HIV and hepatitis B and C. Hepatitis B surface antibodies confirm immunity from vaccination. HIV and hepatitis C are treatable if detected early. Recommended for anyone with history of unprotected sex, needle sharing, or BBV exposure.

name: Gonorrhoea & Chlamydia Test (PCR)
blurb: PCR testing for the two most common bacterial STIs in the UK. Both are often symptom-free, which is why regular screening is recommended by NICE for sexually active people under 25 and those with new or multiple partners. Both are curable with antibiotics when caught early.

name: STI Profile 2 – Core Blood Screen (HIV, Syphilis, Hepatitis)
blurb: Blood-based STI panel covering HIV, syphilis, hepatitis B, and C. Syphilis rates in the UK have risen to their highest since the 1940s. All four infections can be asymptomatic and cause severe long-term harm if untreated.
components: HIV I & II Ab/p24 Ag screen

name: STI Comprehensive Screen (6 pathogens)
blurb: Practical all-in-one STI screen combining PCR for gonorrhoea and chlamydia with blood tests for HIV, syphilis, hepatitis B, and C. Covers six of the most clinically important STIs.
components: HIV I & II Ab/p24 Ag screen

name: STI Routine Screen with Hepatitis B
blurb: Routine STI screen covering gonorrhoea, chlamydia, HIV, and hepatitis B. Suitable for regular sexual health monitoring in sexually active adults.
components: HIV I & II Ab/p24 Ag screen

name: STI Routine Screen with Syphilis
blurb: Covers gonorrhoea, chlamydia, syphilis, and HIV. Given the significant recent rise in syphilis diagnoses in the UK, its inclusion alongside the standard tests is recommended by BASHH guidelines.
components: HIV I & II Ab/p24 Ag screen

name: Early Viral Detection (PCR – HIV & Hepatitis Viral Load)
blurb: PCR detects HIV, hepatitis B, and hepatitis C before antibodies develop. HIV RNA detectable within 10–14 days of infection; standard antibody tests may not be positive for up to 45 days. Most sensitive early-detection test available. Appropriate after high-risk exposure.

name: Needle Stick Injury Screen
blurb: For potential exposure to blood-borne viruses through needlestick, splash, or similar incident. Tests for HIV, hepatitis B, and C. Results guide need for post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) and hepatitis B boosters. NHS: baseline testing as soon as possible after exposure.
components: HIV I & II Ab/p24 Ag screen

name: Lyme Disease Antibody Test
blurb: Tests for antibodies against Borrelia bacteria (cause of Lyme disease, transmitted by ticks). NICE recommends two-tier antibody testing. Appropriate for anyone bitten by a tick or visiting tick habitats in endemic UK areas.
components: Borrelia Antibodies IgG and IgM (Lyme Disease)

name: Hepatitis B Immunity Check (Vaccination Status)
blurb: Measures protective antibodies against hepatitis B to confirm whether vaccination has provided adequate immunity. NHS: ≥10 mIU/mL confirms protection. Needed by healthcare workers, travellers, and those with risk factors for hepatitis B exposure.
